title: The Story of Ferdinand
description: A picture book about a peaceable bull who refuses martial expectations.
descriptionParagraphs: A picture book about a peaceable bull who refuses martial expectations.

The Story of Ferdinand is usually read through its treatment of pacifism, children's literature, and nonconformity. As a children's book, it turns those concerns into conflicts of character, voice, setting, and social pressure rather than leaving them as abstract ideas.

Part of the work's durability lies in the way its form intensifies its themes. Readers return to it not only for subject matter but for the distinctive voice, structure, and atmosphere through which it makes pacifism, children's literature, and nonconformity feel immediate.

overview.englishDescription: A picture book about a peaceable bull who refuses martial expectations.
banningParagraphs: The Story of Ferdinand entered censorship debates as a children's book associated with pacifism, children's literature, and nonconformity. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around anti war and ideological control.

The earliest event currently captured here is 1930s in Germany, where Nazi authorities banned circulation. A gentle pacifist book was unacceptable to a militarized regime. The ban is a useful reminder that censorship also targets tone and values, not only explicit politics.
This entry is still incomplete: more jurisdictions, court orders, and translated justifications should be added over time.

counterReadings.title: The Origins of Totalitarianism
counterReadings.author: Hannah Arendt
counterReadings.note: A foundational analysis of state terror, propaganda, and ideological conformity.

counterReadings.title: On Tyranny
counterReadings.author: Timothy Snyder
counterReadings.note: A short modern guide to resisting authoritarian politics and controlled public discourse.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for comparing older obscenity, heresy, and political bans with modern free-speech disputes.
